text: Adam Rothermel, Little City Big Town Surgery, presenting a robotic right colectomy for a gentleman that has a large tubular adenoma of the cecum that was not endoscopically resectable.
The surgeon performs a high ligation of the oligocolic vessels, preparing to address the large tubular adenoma of the cecum.
The surgeon elevates the iliocolic pedicle and makes an incision just inferior to it, using robotic instruments to carefully manipulate the tissue.
And you can see, or I can see already having watched this several times, I wasn't quite where I should be there. I should have been a little lower.
The surgeon navigates into the correct dissection plane near the terminal ileum, adjusting the approach to a lower position medially.
The surgeon positions the transverse colon to enhance visualization of the root of the ileocolic vessels, providing a clearer view below these vessels during the robotic right colectomy.
The surgeon dissects posteriorly, entering the area and identifying the duodenum, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, identifying the ureter as it is exposed during the procedure.
The surgeon cleans the ileocolic pedicle, preparing to ligate it using a vessel sealer extend device.
A while back I was using the white load on the stapler to take the pedicle, and honestly I think that I've had less issues with oozing or bleeding using the vessel sealer extend.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, extending it cephalad towards the gallbladder and clearing the tissue off the duodenum.
The surgeon drops the transverse colon down and begins mobilizing the hepatic flexure, manipulating the colon to facilitate access and dissection.
The surgeon encounters a significant amount of adhesions to the gallbladder, which are carefully freed. The gallbladder and liver are retracted cephalad to expose the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon observes the anatomical structures, initially having difficulty distinguishing between the duodenum and the transverse colon. The transverse colon appears to extend under the gallbladder, but the separation between these structures is now visible.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, meeting the previously established medial to lateral dissection plane, and transitions to using scissors for further tissue manipulation.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to retract the gallbladder inferiorly and medially after completing the medial to lateral dissection. The focus is now on freeing the lateral attachments of the gallbladder.
The surgeon elevates the terminal ileum while the appendix becomes visible, completing the mobilization of the right colon.
The surgeon cleans the proximal and distal mesentery, focusing on the area near the terminal ileum.
The surgeon identifies the proximal transverse colon and the right branch of the middle colic artery. The artery is cauterized twice before being transected.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) dye to assess the perfusion of the tissue, observing the fluorescence to ensure adequate blood flow.
The surgeon places the freed specimen aside and prepares to turn towards the anastomosis site.
The surgeon aligns the bowel for a side-to-side isoparastaltic anastomosis using a 60mm stapler. A white load is used for the anastomosis, while a blue load is used to transect the bowel.

The surgeon inserts a stapler through the suprapubic port, preparing for an anastomosis. The port will be extended with an abbreviated fan and steel incision for specimen extraction. The surgeon carefully releases tension from the retraction suture to prevent the stapler from pushing the bowel tissue during the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Loc suture to close the common channel, initially stitching in a pattern of in-to-out and out-to-in. A mistake occurs when the surgeon inadvertently stitches in the same direction twice, causing tissue oozing. The surgeon then transitions to a canal stitch to resolve the issue.
The surgeon runs a suture along the anastomosis site, imbricating the tissue, and then applies a reinforcement stitch using silk to suspend the colon.

This guy did very well. He went home the morning after surgery. I do not have his pathology back yet. But again, we're assuming it's benign. And I'll see him this week. Thank you so much for watching. I really appreciate it.
